item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the Jack Parsons character, and the idea of a sex-powered rocket.
Oh, and the ending was pretty nice too.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except Jack Parsons was a real legit rocket scientist / occultist who worked with Crowley and this does have the stuff associated with Thelema.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddamn it, Hubbard was onto something. Was he part of the Pentagram or was his 'pet project' the no-go?

On the one hand I'm disappointed that the actual nature and origin of the Moon Anomaly and the involvement of the Department is pretty much skimmed over - the premise of the SCP gets kind of lost beneath the Thelema stuff, but the final reveal is just so good that it's still a +1.

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: Sheer lunacy.

item.title: Re: I dont entirely get this
item.content:encoded:
The Moon that we call the Moon is actually hollow; it's all one giant prison for some sort of eldritch abomination. Jack Parsons pretty much recognized it was bad news.

No idea what Crowley was doing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I strongly appreciate the establishment of literal big dick energy into the scp universe. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think he was trying to indulge with the goddess sealed within the Moon…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking incredible, this is exactly what I love about SCP, +1 to the Moon and beyond.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lol

item.title: I dont entirely get this
item.content:encoded: I don't see what is wrong with the moon here, could someone explain it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the Moon is a containment unit for a broken god. That doesn't really explain how this connects to Aleister Crowley putting his orgasm face on the dark side, though.

item.title: Re: Crowley's Bizarre Adventure
item.content:encoded:
That wikipedia link alone has moved this from a "started out promising but quickly turned to a lolfoundation tale of why the moon landings had to be faked" to "actually pretty fucking incredible" for me. All the stuff that seemed bananas? Actually true, or at least part of the lore which the three suspects pictured in the file were jointly involved in IRL. And yeah, the page is but a reformatting away from being an SCP.

Though with the whole hollowing-out-the-moon-and-leaving-geometric-shapes-behind schtick, I'm still slightly put out it didn't turn out that the far side of the moon is covered with an extensive array of Steven Universe style Gem-tech and architecture, maybe a massive interstellar transmitter or something, but just a Mighty Boosh style face. As horrific and hilarious the mental image of said face actually is.
Cool moon. Good work, you two.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Shoot for the moon - even if you miss, you will land among the stars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like how effectively this article was able to go from humorous to dark, in that specific order. I guess it's something only a topic like sex can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was on the fence about this until the last line.
It gave me a good chuckle +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait..but..lunar area 32…. on scp-4000. can’t compute (no vote I think)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is about where I'm coming from, too. This was super fun to read, overall, so I'm upvoting, but I feel like this could've been both trimmed down and pushed further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't quite work for me on the whole. There's lots of good stuff here, but it runs a little too long and has a few too many moving parts (the department of abnormalities doesn't ultimately need to be here and I wish the office-block didn't have to share the spotlight with all the other weirdness which is going on here), and the ending is a punchline rather than a proper conclusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this and I must fuck the moon I MUST TRANSLATE THIS!!!
I think Parsons and Crowley would be pleased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All it was missing was a Kubrick mention.

Fixed.

Under Protocol ECLIPSE EYES WIDE SHUT, the following provisions are to be met:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This probably won't win the contest, but I won't be unhappy if it does. Such a great intersection of absurdist humor, supernaturalism, and clinical soviet mad science mixed with moon landing conspiracies. All it was missing was a Kubrick mention. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. There's something about the entries that deal with old hucksters and occultists like Crowley and such (see "Garfield minus Garfield" for another contest entry that I loved) that really gets me, and even though I hate the real people there's an appeal to fictionalized version. Moreover, the execution here is buck wild and takes us through a whirlwind of 60s zeitgeist that was really fun to read.

The humor was great, too. A lot of people are saying this should be a -J and I think that's a shame. An SCP should make you feel something, yes, but it doesn't always have to be fear. It can be funny. I really think -Js are better suited to parodies of existing articles and the format at large, not to well executed, standalone, humorous articles that still fit the context of what an SCP should be, and this fits the bill.

Hearty upvote, good luck in the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what the fuck

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haha!

I love it. The interview with Parsons is not only one of the best tools of delivering exposition en masse that I have seen on the Wiki, its also a really great interview, being fast paced and oozing with character. Parsons practically jumps out of the screen at the reader you do such a great job with characterization there.

On top of that, the plan at the end to fake the moon landing using SCP-1812 is a particularly nifty means of ending the article.

+1. This article was nuts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, but I'm not sure if I can. It seems like it's building up 4000-2 as something big and scary, and I guess, technically, it is, just not in the way I would have expected. It's like I was eating a nice big plate of horror, but then someone spilled their dick jokes all over it halfway through. I like each of the elements individually, but not the way they were put together here.

On a more technical note, Addenda 4000.2 and 4000.3 should really be in collapsibles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that's quite understandable — this piece is far away from what I'd typically do. It was an experiment for me; I like collapsing ideas, while ARD likes expanding them. Either approach can lead to great articles — but this was an attempt to see if those approaches could cohabitate.

I'm not too worried about down-votes, here. I mean, I shouldn't be worried anyway, I know. I'm especially not worried about them in this case, though. I enjoyed writing it; it is way outside my usual comfort zone, and it was nice to stretch into a space I don't typically occupy.

But I'm going to be honest: I would be deeply confused if this actually won the 4k contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The pacing of this one is… weird, to me? I found this to have far too many moving parts for me to really have an experience reading it. First, you have the obviously above-the-paygrade-ness of the Department of Abnormalities. Then, you have the expungated description of the far side of the moon. Then, you have the seance with the crash landing dude who talks about scientology and sex demons and stuff. Then you have the russians exploring the DoA thing before, finally, the moon has an orgasm.

There are enough parts I like that I can't downvote this, but this doesn't really come together for me as a satisfying experience. This just might be me; I'll come back to this later and see if it's just because I'm sleep deprived. Until then, I'm leaving it with a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my favorite obscure Crowley facts: he warned Parsons about Hubbard, saying he was bad news. The idea of young L. Ron and old Aleister having a face to face confrontation is the stuff of fever dreams; alas, it never happened.

I quite liked this - the interaction of thaumaturgy and technology is fascinating when done well, and this was done well to my taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like many of the sexual jokes overstayed their welcome, which is a shame as this contains some nice ideas.

No-Vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Kole Adam and DrAzra. This started off really great with a premise that grabbed me. Things we know hiding something sinister is quite compelling. However, Addendum 4000.2 took me right out of it. The transition from seriousness to crude humour is abrupt and jarring and left me disappointed that you didn't continue down a more serious path.
Excellent! I'm pleased to see this article taking off. Here's to +100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Boy I wish I could upvote comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im not feeling this one. Weird abnormality facilities work for me when they are in a hidden building or some other places, not so much when they are the entire moon, the strange connection to sex gods doesn't work for me either. It feels like two completely different ideas mashed together and they don't work very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, but I think this would work better as a -J

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pure gold. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OH. I had no idea that one didn't stick around. Explains why this is familiar! (God, I'm bad at this.)

+ Ramblings as I read
– hide block

Now that I can approach this on its own merits… I still feel like the Department of Abnormalities isn't a thing that really needs more than one article? Like, seriously, guys, c'mon? People complained about dado saturation, after all; and now I realize they basically all take the form of "building full of cells".

Also, holy shit, this is long.

You've got a really great hook with that redaction immediately following the first explanation of -2, though. Loving "The Pentagram".

Oh my god, this seance interview is utter bugnutty perfection. I love that the interviewer is just completely out of his depth. This guy was nuts! And… it sounds like he's not actually dead? It seemed at first like he might have died in the crash, but he went up as a ghost? (Yeah, that becomes clear by the end. What.)

It will be a Russian who takes the first step across the moon's surface. History will not care whether he was alive or dead.

This is the stuff dreams are made of. These letters are excellent.

Wow, that was fucked up. I can absolutely ignore my issues with the single instance of the DoA and upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: “And then he fucked off to the moon”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As stated in both author posts, it’s a semi-rewrite of psul’s SCP-3001-EX.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've got a 19YY in there?

I have to know, though. I swear to god we've already got A) an "SCP is the Moon" article, B) an article where the interior of the moon is hollow and supported by a hexagonal lattice of something. I very specifically remember those details, and it's not often I specifically remember things. Is this a rewrite or something? I can't keep reading until I know for sure, it's driving me crazy. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And to think, Clef's entry was the more sexually engaging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Souls undergoing atomic fission? I'm afraid I have to question the conclusions of Jack Parson's Ghost on this. Can he cite sources?

Hubbard, L. Ron. Dianetics: The Modern Science of Mental Health. Hermitage House, 1950.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Souls undergoing atomic fission? I'm afraid I have to question the conclusions of Jack Parson's Ghost on this. Can he cite sources?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m glad someone else had the idea to do the Moon landing. I had a somewhat different approach in mind, but this is probably more vibrant and interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus fuck between Bush vs. Gore, Taboo, and this, is the theme "History", or just "Going Fucking Bananas," huh?

This is bonkers!

item.title: Re: theonuclear fuel is stored in the balls
theonuclear fuel is stored in the balls
item.content:encoded: theonuclear fuel is stored in the balls

item.title: Re: Crowley's Bizarre Adventure
item.content:encoded:
YOOOOOOOO

First off, huge thank you to The Great Hippo for being a wonderful coauthor and making this so much better than I could have ever done by myself!

Second off, I want to dedicate this one to psul, who graciously allowed me to rewrite and use material from his original SCP-3001-EX. It was before its time and I hope we did it justice.

Thirdly, special thanks to everyone who looked this over and gave it the thumbs up or down.

Fourth - this one is for you, Jack Parsons. You were before your time too. Shine on, you crazy sex diamond.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And so, at Crowley's bidding, I haunted the nosecone of Helios 1 on its maiden voyage!

This is the greatest line that anyone has ever used in anything.

This is a perfect riff on the weird and surreal history of the occult, both ludicrous and surprisingly informative at the same time, and all dripping with entertaining character.

At Crowley's bidding, I upvote this article.

item.title: Crowley's Bizarre Adventure
item.content:encoded:
And all that is now
And all that is gone
And all that's to come
And everything under the sun
is in tune

But the sun is eclipsed
by the Moon ~

SOURCES:

  • Images are here, here, here, and here.
  • Based on the life and times of Jack Parsons, who's wikipedia article qualifies as an SCP entry on its own.

FEEDBACK:

  • Peppersghost (Taboo)
  • Mortos (Wormwood)
  • djkaktus (Bush vs Gore)
  • Captain Kirby (Sometimes I Look At The Sky So I Can Feel Small)
  • Roget (History, Together)
  • Weizhong (Chronos)
  • Jekeled (Tower)
  • Taffeta (Allison Eckhart)
  • TyGently (Laika's Sweetheart Space-Beacon)
  • tawny0
  • ch00bakka (Death of the Artist)
  • Soulless (Real Life Memorial For A Virtual Non-Existence)
  • kinchtheknifeblade (A Dream Come True)
  • Special thanks to psul (Modified Hospital Beds) for allowing us to do this rewrite of his excellent SCP-3001-EX.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!

Or click here for more of A Random Day's articles!

(There is no dark side in the moon really
Matter of fact it's all dark)
